era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-4th century BCE, dominated by the rivalry between Sparta and Thebes following the Theban hegemony established after Leuctra (371 BCE). Philip II of Macedon was consolidating power in the north. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II remained the dominant Near Eastern power, though facing internal instability. In China, the Warring States period was intensifying, with major states consolidating territory and military innovation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek/Roman contact zones
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited Greek sources)

events.title: Theban hegemony consolidates; Pelopidas leads Theban expansion
events.description: Following the Theban victory at Leuctra in 371 BCE, Pelopidas and Epaminondas continued to expand Theban influence across Greece. In 365 BCE, Pelopidas led a military campaign into Thessaly to secure Theban dominance in northern Greece and counter Macedonian expansion. This period saw Thebes at its zenith as a Greek power, though internal tensions and the rise of Philip II would soon undermine this hegemony.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's account is near-contemporary but ends around 362 BCE. Later sources like Plutarch provide narrative detail but are written centuries later. The general trajectory of Theban expansion is secure; specific events in 365 BCE are less precisely dated.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Artaxerxes II faces internal rebellion; Egyptian revolt continues
events.description: The reign of Artaxerxes II (ruled 404–358 BCE) was marked by chronic instability. In 365 BCE, Egypt remained in open revolt under native pharaohs (the 30th Dynasty), having thrown off Persian control around 380 BCE. Simultaneously, Artaxerxes faced rebellions from satraps and dynastic rivals. The empire's military capacity, though still formidable, was increasingly stretched. This period foreshadowed the empire's vulnerability to Macedonian conquest under Alexander.

sources.name: Egyptian temple inscriptions and king lists

events.certainty_note: Egyptian independence is well-attested epigraphically. The dating of specific Persian internal crises in 365 BCE is less precise; Greek sources are our main record and are sometimes anecdotal.

events.title: Philip II of Macedon consolidates northern Greece
events.description: Philip II, who became king of Macedon around 359 BCE, was rapidly transforming Macedon from a peripheral kingdom into a major power. By 365 BCE, he had secured his throne against rivals and was beginning military campaigns to expand Macedonian territory and influence. His military reforms—the sarissa phalanx and professional standing army—were revolutionary. This period marks the beginning of Macedonian ascendancy that would culminate in Alexander's conquests.
events.certainty_note: Philip's reign is well-documented in later Greek and Roman sources, but contemporary accounts are sparse. The chronology of his early campaigns is sometimes uncertain by a few years.

events.title: Warring States period intensifies in China; Qin and Chu expand
events.description: During the Warring States period (475–221 BCE), the major Chinese states were engaged in continuous military competition and territorial consolidation. Around 365 BCE, the state of Qin in the west and Chu in the south were expanding their power. Qin was beginning the military and administrative reforms under Shang Yang (who served the state of Wei before moving to Qin around 361 BCE) that would eventually enable Qin's unification of China. This era saw rapid innovation in military technology, statecraft, and philosophy.
key_figures: Shang Yang (Legalist reformer, active in Wei and Qin)
Various rulers of Qin, Chu, Zhao, and other states

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological evidence from Warring States sites

events.certainty_note: Chinese historical chronology for the Warring States period is uncertain by several years. The Shiji was compiled centuries later (c. 109 BCE). Archaeological evidence provides material confirmation but not precise dating for individual years.

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of Pelopidas' Thessalian campaigns is debated; some scholars place key events in 364 or 366 BCE rather than 365 BCE. The chronology of Philip II's early reign is also uncertain—some sources suggest his consolidation of power took longer than traditional accounts indicate. For China, the Warring States chronology is notoriously uncertain; the Bamboo Annals and Shiji sometimes disagree by several years on the same events. No major events listed above are fundamentally disputed, but their precise dating should be treated as approximate.
historiographic_note: The year 365 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Greek world through later compilations (Plutarch, Diodorus, Xenophon's Hellenica), though contemporary sources are limited. For Persia and Egypt, we rely on Greek accounts and Egyptian temple inscriptions, which may be biased or incomplete. Chinese sources are entirely post-hoc compilations with uncertain chronology. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no surviving written records for this year. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East.

graph_edges.note: Theban hegemony under Pelopidas and Epaminondas culminated in the Battle of Mantinea (362 BCE), where Epaminondas died and Theban dominance ended.
Philip II's consolidation of Macedonian power in the 360s–350s BCE led to his victory at Chaeronea (338 BCE), which made Macedon the dominant Greek power.
The Egyptian revolt and Persian instability in the 360s BCE eventually led to Artaxerxes III's reconquest of Egypt in 343 BCE.
The intensification of Warring States competition in China was contemporary with Shang Yang's arrival in Qin and the beginning of his reforms.
